name: Dun Aengus
description: Dún Aonghasa (Dun Aengus) is a prehistoric hill fort located on Inishmore, one of the Aran Islands in County Galway, Ireland. Perched dramatically on the edge of a 300-foot cliff overlooking the Atlantic Ocean, it is one of the most iconic and best-preserved examples of an Irish stone fort. While its construction dates back to the Bronze Age and Iron Age, its mythological significance is interwoven with the legends of the Fir Bolg, one of the early races to inhabit Ireland. Legend states that Aengus, a chieftain or king of the Fir Bolg, retreated to this fort after being defeated by the Tuatha Dé Danann, the divine race of Irish mythology. The fort served as a defensive stronghold, a symbol of resistance, and a place of refuge. Some believe that the fort held spiritual significance, possibly used for ritualistic practices or ceremonies. The sheer scale of the structure and its strategic location suggest a place of power and importance, both militarily and symbolically. The rugged landscape surrounding Dún Aonghasa further adds to its mystique and enduring appeal, cementing its place within the rich tapestry of Irish mythology and folklore. Its presence evokes a sense of ancient power and the enduring connection between the land, its people, and their legendary past. The fort represents not just a physical structure but a tangible link to the mythological history of Ireland and the struggles of its early inhabitants.
subtitle: Ancient Stone Fort on the Aran Islands
associatedDeities: None specifically linked, but associated with pre-Celtic deities and spirits of the land

keyMyths.title: Aengus and the Fir Bolg
keyMyths.description: Legend states that Aengus, a leader of the Fir Bolg, used Dun Aengus as a refuge after being defeated by the Tuatha Dé Danann. The fort became a symbol of their resistance against the invading deities.

keyMyths.title: Spiritual Significance of the Fort
keyMyths.description: Some believe that Dun Aengus served as a sacred space for rituals and ceremonies, connecting the inhabitants to the spirits of the land and their ancestors. Its strategic location and imposing structure suggest a place of power and significance.

sources.author: T.F. O'Rahilly
sources.description: A comprehensive study of early Irish history and mythology, providing context for the Fir Bolg and their role.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of early Irish history and mythology, providing context for the Fir Bolg and their role.
